examples.title: Basic Production System Interpreter
examples.description: Demonstrates the core Recognize-Act cycle of a production system
examples.code:
PRODUCTION-SYSTEM()
  WorkingMemory ← []  // Initial empty WM
  ProductionMemory ← LOAD-RULES()  // Load all production rules
  
  REPEAT
    // MATCH PHASE
    ActivatedRules ← []
    FOR EACH rule IN ProductionMemory
      IF MATCH-CONDITIONS(rule.conditions, WorkingMemory) THEN
        ADD rule TO ActivatedRules
      END IF
    END FOR
    
    // CONFLICT RESOLUTION PHASE
    IF ActivatedRules IS EMPTY THEN
      RETURN FAILURE  // No rules can fire
    END IF
    SelectedRule ← CONFLICT-RESOLUTION(ActivatedRules)
    
    // ACT PHASE
    EXECUTE-ACTIONS(SelectedRule.actions)
    
  UNTIL Goal-Reached() OR ActivatedRules IS EMPTY
  RETURN SUCCESS
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the three-phase cycle: MATCH finds all rules whose conditions match current facts, CONFLICT RESOLUTION selects one rule when multiple rules match, and ACT executes the selected rule's actions on working memory.
examples.timeComplexity: O(R × F × C) - where R=rules, F=facts, C=conditions per rule
examples.spaceComplexity: O(W + P) - where W=working memory size, P=production memory size

examples.title: Forward Chaining Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation of forward chaining algorithm for a simple production system
examples.explanation: This Python code implements forward chaining. It starts with initial facts in working memory and repeatedly finds rules whose conditions are satisfied, executing their actions to add new facts. The process continues until no more rules can fire or the goal is reached.
examples.timeComplexity: O(R × I) where R=rules, I=inferences until fixpoint
examples.spaceComplexity: O(W + R) where W=working memory, R=number of rules

examples.title: Backward Chaining Implementation
examples.description: Java implementation of backward chaining for goal-driven reasoning
examples.explanation: This Java implementation demonstrates backward chaining (goal-driven reasoning). It starts with a goal hypothesis and recursively tries to prove all required conditions. If a condition can't be proven from facts or rules, it backtracks and tries another rule.
examples.timeComplexity: O(B^d) where B=average branches, d=depth of proof tree
examples.spaceComplexity: O(d) for recursion stack plus O(W) for working memory
